title: Overriding and Super 🦸
description: Modify inherited methods and elegantly summon parent code using super.

slides.title: Rebellion! 🎸
slides.content:
When a Child inherits a method from a Parent, does it have to keep the exact original behavior?

No!

A Child can completely **Override** an inherited method simply by flawlessly redefining it inside its own class.

slides.title: Overriding a Method
slides.content: Run this! Both animals inherited `speak`, but the `Dog` rebelled and cleanly overwrote the parent's default behavior with its own.
slides.codeSnippet:
class Animal
  def speak
    puts "Hello!"
  end
end

class Dog < Animal
  # We explicitly overwrite the Parent's code
  def speak
    puts "Woof woof!"
  end
end

animal = Animal.new
dog = Dog.new

animal.speak
dog.speak

quizData.question: How do you forcefully override an inherited method?

options.text: You physically delete the method entirely from the parent class.
You just define a brand new method inside the Child class that has the exact same name.

quizData.explanation: Ruby perfectly natively looks at the Child class first. If it finds the method there, it runs it immediately and never bothers checking the Parent.

slides.title: The Super Keyword 🦸
slides.content:
Sometimes you don't want to completely destroy the parent's code. You just want to strictly *add onto it*.

You can explicitly summon the parent's method logic from INSIDE your child's overridden method using the magical keyword `super`.

When Ruby hits `super`, it instantly runs the parent's exact code, then returns to finish the child's code!

slides.title: Super in Action
slides.content: Run this! See how `super` seamlessly grabs the Parent's logic, pulls it down, and beautifully weaves it perfectly into the Child's logic.
slides.codeSnippet:
class Parent
  def greet
    puts "I am the Parent."
  end
end

class Child < Parent
  def greet
    super # This runs the Parent's code right now!
    puts "And I am the Child."
  end
end

son = Child.new
son.greet

quizData.question: What is the primary function of the `super` keyword inside an overridden method?

options.text: It literally calls the Parent's original version of the method to run, merging the behaviors.
It makes the method immensely faster.

quizData.explanation: Using `super` allows a Child class to perfectly stack its own custom logic right on top of the Parent's existing logic, minimizing code repetition flawlessly.
